title: Correcting a Faulty Primality Test using Miller-Rabin Algorithm

statement.markdown:
Write a function to test if a given number `n` is prime or not. Implement the Miller-Rabin primality test using the following algorithm.

The input `n` is a very large number (it's not given in the problem, but you may assume it's a very large number)

Your function should return `True` if `n` is prime and `False` otherwise.

The Miller-Rabin primality test is based on the following theorem: given a prime number `p`, for any integer `a` such that `1 <= a <= p-1`, `a^(p-1) % p = 1`. This theorem is the basis of the Miller-Rabin algorithm.

However, the Miller-Rabin algorithm is an probabilistic algorithm and it's not a deterministic algorithm. This means that even if `n` is composite, there's still a small chance (approximately 1 in 4^k) that the test will return `True`, even if `n` is not prime.

This question is a bug-fix question. You are given a starter code, but it has some bugs that you need to fix. Please fix the bugs and make the function work correctly.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a very large number n
statement.outputFormat: Return True if n is prime, False otherwise
statement.notes: Miller-Rabin algorithm is a probabilistic algorithm to test the primality of a number. It's not a deterministic algorithm.

constraints.description: Input number to check for primality

hints.text: The Miller-Rabin algorithm is a probabilistic algorithm to test the primality of a number. It's not a deterministic algorithm.

editorial.intuition: The Miller-Rabin algorithm is an extension of the Fermat's little theorem.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach to test the primality of a number is not efficient for very large numbers.
editorial.betterSolution: The Miller-Rabin algorithm is a probabilistic algorithm to test the primality of a number.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the Miller-Rabin algorithm to test the primality of a number.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness of the Miller-Rabin algorithm can be found in any textbook of number theory.
editorial.dryRun: Dry run of the Miller-Rabin algorithm can be done by testing the primality of a small number.
commonMistakes: Not checking the input number for primality
Not using the correct number of iterations
